name: Luke Bhan
tagline: I build algorithms for controlling physical systems that combine machine learning with structure to provide provable guarantees.
blurb: Pure learning is fast but unaccountable; classical control is rigorous but requires domain expertise and lacks scalability. My research fuses their strengths, building ultra-fast controllers for robots, vehicles, and biological systems that act in real time and provide theoretic performance guarantees.
availability: I am on the academic job market this year and excited to find the right fit. I'd love to connect, reach me at lbhan@ucsd.edu.

left.label: no predictor
left.alt: A system with input delay and no compensation: the response oscillates and fails to settle.

right.label: neural-operator predictor
right.alt: The same delayed system with a neural-operator predictor in the loop: the response is stabilized and settles.

homeVision.lead: Algorithms that capitalize on the performance of machine learning, with the provable certificates needed for deployment in real-world physical systems. I tell an engineer exactly how accurately to train their controller and, from the loss it reaches, the closed-loop performance it will achieve.

clips.label: Vehicle navigation
clips.blurb: A car-like vehicle has different delays on its steering and acceleration. A separate neural-operator predictor compensates each one, so it still drives cleanly to its target.

papers.title: Delay Compensation of Multi-Input Distinct Delay Nonlinear Systems via Neural Operators
Stabilization of Nonlinear Systems with Unknown Delays via Delay-Adaptive Neural Operator Approximate Predictors

clips.label: Life Sciences
clips.blurb: An age-structured population is steered to a target equilibrium, with the stabilizing control gains learned online from the population's birth and mortality rates.

papers.title: Lotka–Sharpe Neural Operators for Control of Population PDEs
PDE Control Gym: A Benchmark for Data-Driven Boundary Control

clips.label: Predictor feedback
clips.blurb: A manipulator performs a fast task through a long actuation delay. Without compensation the delay destabilizes the loop and the task fails; a neural-operator predictor reconstructs the future state so the same task completes, with a stability guarantee.

papers.title: Neural Operators for Predictor Feedback Control of Nonlinear Delay Systems

clips.label: PDE backstepping
clips.blurb: Stabilizing a PDE from its boundary requires solving a kernel PDE for the control gains, recomputed for every new plant. A neural operator learns that gain-kernel map directly, preserving the stability guarantee while replacing the expensive solve, and even adapting it online when the plant's coefficients are unknown.

papers.title: Neural Operators for Bypassing Gain and Control Computations in PDE Backstepping
Adaptive Control of Reaction–Diffusion PDEs via Neural Operator-Approximated Gain Kernels

cta.label: Check out more of my research during my Ph.D.

researchPage.title: Algorithms that capitalize on the performance of machine learning with the provable certificates needed for deployment in real-world physical systems
researchPage.lead: The power of modern neural networks is clear, but physical systems cannot go into the real world without guarantees on performance and safety. My research makes black-box neural networks deployable for real systems: I tell the engineer exactly how accurately to train their controller and, from the loss it reaches, the closed-loop performance it will achieve, and how gracefully that degrades as the network's error grows.

thrusts.title: Design of deployable ML systems, with closed-loop performance guarantees
thrusts.idea: This is the engine behind everything else I do. Classical control earns its guarantees by solving heavy numerical routines, such as backstepping kernel PDEs or predictor integrals, that are provably correct but far too slow to recompute online. I showed these computations are operators a neural network can learn, and proved the learned approximation preserves the closed-loop stability guarantee. The expensive solve collapses into a single forward pass and the proof travels with it: control at machine-learning speed, on real systems, without giving up certification.
thrusts.explain: The contribution is the certificate, not the network: given your system and your data, the theory says how accurate the operator must be to remain stable, and the smaller that error, the tighter the performance you can prove.

papers.title: Neural Operators for Bypassing Gain and Control Computations in PDE Backstepping
Neural Operators of Backstepping Controller and Observer Gains
Adaptive Neural-Operator Backstepping Control of a Benchmark Hyperbolic PDE
Stabilization of Nonlinear Systems with Unknown Delays via Delay-Adaptive Neural Operator Approximate Predictors

items.label: backstepping kernels
items.caption: A neural operator learns the backstepping gain kernel that stabilizes the PDE, reproducing the exact kernel while replacing a costly numerical solve with a single forward pass that runs orders of magnitude faster.
items.paper: Neural Operators for Bypassing Gain and Control Computations in PDE Backstepping

items.label: adaptive control
items.caption: Adaptive control of a reaction–diffusion PDE: as the unknown reaction parameters are identified online, the neural operator re-evaluates the stabilizing gain kernel instantly at each step, keeping the closed loop provably stable as it learns.
items.paper: Adaptive Control of Reaction–Diffusion PDEs via Neural Operator-Approximated Gain Kernels

thrusts.title: Autonomy that generalizes, with guarantees via operator learning
thrusts.idea: Robots break the clean assumptions controllers are designed under: they act through real communication and actuation delays, and they have to work in environments they never saw in training. I address both with operator learning. A planning neural operator learns the cost-to-go for navigation and generalizes to maps far finer than the ones it trained on, while a learned predictor reconstructs the delayed state in a single forward pass so the controller acts on time, carrying the same stability guarantee as the classical predictor it replaces. Both results rest on the same scientific-machine-learning principle: I encode the problem's structure into the network itself (e.g. the triangle inequality explicitly enforced in the neural net design), so performance and certifiable deployment come built in.
thrusts.explain: Real manipulators act on commands that arrive late; a learned predictor reconstructs the future state so the controller acts on time, with a proof that the closed loop stays stable.

papers.title: Generalizable Motion Planning via Operator Learning
Neural Operators for Predictor Feedback Control of Nonlinear Delay Systems
Sampling-Horizon Neural Operator Predictors for Nonlinear Control
Concurrent Policy Blending and System Identification for Generalized Assistive Control

items.label: assistive control
items.caption: Blending learned policies with online system identification so a robot can adapt to each person it assists.
items.paper: Concurrent Policy Blending and System Identification for Generalized Assistive Control

thrusts.title: Traffic, biology, and more applications of neural operator control design
thrusts.idea: The same machinery reaches well beyond the PDEs it started on. When a vehicle receives several control inputs that each arrive after a different delay, I assign one neural-operator predictor per input, compensating all the distinct delays at once with a stability guarantee. For biological populations, where the stabilizing gains depend on birth and mortality rates that are unknown and must be identified online, a neural operator learns the control-design map and re-evaluates it instantly as those estimates update, steering an age-structured colony to its target equilibrium. To let others build here, I co-created PDEControlGym, an open, documented benchmark for data-driven boundary control spanning physics, biology, and medicine, supported by an NVIDIA Academic Grant.
thrusts.explain: PDEControlGym, supported by an NVIDIA Academic Grant, gives the community a standard testbed for data-driven boundary control: the infrastructure that lets these methods spread beyond any single lab.

papers.title: Delay Compensation of Multi-Input Distinct Delay Nonlinear Systems via Neural Operators
Lotka–Sharpe Neural Operators for Control of Population PDEs
PDE Control Gym: A Benchmark for Data-Driven Boundary Control

items.paper: Delay Compensation of Multi-Input Distinct Delay Nonlinear Systems via Neural Operators

items.paper: Lotka–Sharpe Neural Operators for Control of Population PDEs

aboutPage.identity: PhD candidate at UC San Diego, working where scientific machine learning meets control theory.
aboutPage.bio: I build algorithms that control real-world systems, pairing the speed of machine learning with the guarantees of control theory. Specifically, I design neural operators with provable guarantees for systems ranging from PDEs and robots to biological systems. I'm thankful to be advised by Yuanyuan Shi and Miroslav Krstić, and supported by the DOE Computational Science Graduate Fellowship.
story: I build algorithms that control real-world systems by combining the speed of machine learning with the guarantees of control theory. My aim is a single, rigorous toolkit that transfers across domains instead of being rebuilt for each one, and that is trustworthy enough to run inside the feedback loop.

I came to control from physics. In an accelerated bachelor's and master's at Vanderbilt University, I worked across physics, applied mathematics, and computer science, simulating ultrafast laser-driven electron dynamics with Kálmán Varga and studying reinforcement learning and robotics with Gautam Biswas.

Those two threads, rigorous physical modeling and learning for decision-making, converged into the question that drives my research today: can modern learning be made trustworthy enough to place inside a controller? My master's thesis on deep reinforcement learning for adaptive control was a first step, and my doctorate turned it into a program.

At UC San Diego I am advised by Yuanyuan Shi and Miroslav Krstić and supported by the DOE Computational Science Graduate Fellowship. I develop neural operators with provable control-theoretic guarantees, fast enough to run in real time, for systems ranging from PDEs and robots to biological systems. Alongside the research I help organize the community's first workshop on AI for PDE control, and I care about teaching and broadening participation in the field.

timeline.detail: I did an accelerated B.S./M.S. majoring in physics, applied mathematics, and computer science. At Vandy, I made my first exploration into research, working on laser-driven electron dynamics, and took my first steps in reinforcement learning and robotics. I also learned to code the old-fashioned way (without Claude or GPT), interning at both T-Mobile and MongoDB. I will forever cherish my Vanderbilt years!
Ph.D. in Electrical & Computer Engineering with Yuanyuan Shi and Miroslav Krstić, developing neural operators with control-theoretic guarantees. DOE Computational Science Graduate Fellow.
Randomized linear-algebra solvers for distributed nonconvex optimization.
Custom LLMs adjusting time-series demand forecasts across 30M+ products.
Research scientist intern building foundation models for the power grid.

teachingPage.philosophy: In the ChatGPT era, I think the most valuable part of a classroom is the people in it, the peers and colleagues you get to think alongside. So in the lectures I help teach, I try to lean on participation and discussion rather than one-way delivery. I have mentored seven undergraduate and master's researchers. Three became publication coauthors, and three former mentees subsequently entered graduate programs at ETH Zurich, UC San Diego, and the University of Wisconsin.

series.title: Scientific machine learning: a mini lecture series
series.intro: I taught several lectures within Physics-Informed Machine Learning at UC San Diego, introducing the core ideas of scientific machine learning. The full lectures are on YouTube; here is a quick tour of what each one covers. These are recordings of live classes, so student questions and discussion are woven throughout. In the participation-first spirit above, they are real teaching sessions rather than polished videos.

lectures.title: Neural ODEs
lectures.summary: How treating a network's depth as continuous time turns it into a dynamical system that an ODE solver integrates and the adjoint method trains efficiently.

clips.label: From ResNet to a neural ODE

lectures.title: Neural operators
lectures.summary: Learning maps between function spaces rather than between fixed vectors, so one model transfers across discretizations. Builds up to DeepONet and the Fourier Neural Operator.

lectures.title: Physics-informed neural networks
lectures.summary: Embedding a PDE's residual directly in the training loss so a network can solve or identify differential equations with little or no data, and where the approach tends to break down.

lectures.title: Foundation models
lectures.summary: What foundation models are, how pretraining at scale produces general-purpose representations, and what their rise means for scientific computing.

clips.label: Why physics is harder than NLP
